items.title: Foreign verdicts must be weighed against domestic standards: IHC
items.description: • Justice Minhas rules against enforcement of Dubai decree • Judgement notes criminal findings do not bind civil courts, calls for balance between global and local principles ISLAMABAD: Dismissing a petition filed by the Gulf-based arm of a major technology company, the Islamabad High Court stresses the need to strike a balance between the principle of international comity and the protections afforded by domestic law when enforcing foreign courts’ decrees. The ruling came in a case filed by two Pakistani businessmen, Latif Hakeem and another, through their counsel Kashif Ali Malik, against the execution of a money decree obtained by a technology company from Dubai Court of First Instance in October 2021. The decree was subsequently upheld by the Dubai Court of Appeal in June 2022, and affirmed by the Dubai Court of Cassation in October 2022. The high court set aside the July 2025 order of a local executing court, which had allowed the enforcement of the Dubai decree, while dismissing the execution petition filed under Section 44-A of the Code of Civil Procedure. In his order, Justice Raja Inaam Ameen Minhas ruled that determining civil liability based on criminal findings deprives litigants of independent adjudication essential to due process. Justice Minhas observed that courts must independently determine whether foreign judgements satisfy Pakistani legal standards regarding jurisdiction, natural justice, fraud, public policy, and domestic law compliance. The ruling clarified that while Section 44-A permitted execution of decrees from reciprocating territories like the UAE, it remained subject to exceptions under Section 13 of the Civil Procedure Code (CPC). If any exception applies, the foreign judgement loses its conclusiveness and cannot be enforced. Civil vs criminal proceedings The core issue that proved decisive was that Dubai courts had substantially relied upon findings from criminal proceedings while determining the appellants’ civil liability, which was fundamentally inconsistent with settled Pakistani jurisprudence. Civil and criminal proceedings operate independently. Criminal convictions require proof beyond reasonable doubt, while civil liability is determined on preponderance of probabilities, the order read. Citing the Supreme Court’s binding judgement in Karachi Transport Corporation v. Muhammad Hanif, the court reiterated that criminal findings do not bind civil courts. By virtue of Article 189 of the Constitution, this principle binds all Pakistani courts, including executing courts dealing with foreign decrees. The counsel for Samsung Gulf Electronics FZE argued that the Dubai court had also relied on an expert report, not solely on criminal findings. The court, however, rejected this contention, holding that “the presence of an expert report does not remove the legal defect arising from the reliance placed upon the criminal judgement”. “Once the findings of a criminal court become one of the foundations for determining civil rights and liabilities, the legality of such reliance must be examined in the light of the settled principles of law applicable in Pakistan,” the judgement stated. The court found the foreign judgement attracted exceptions under Section 13(c) and (f) of the CPC, which apply where a judgement refuses to recognise Pakistani law or sustains a claim founded on breach of Pakistani law. The court also invoked Article 10-A of the Constitution, which guarantees fair trial and due process. Determining civil liability based on criminal findings deprives litigants of independent adjudication essential to due process, it added. Since the decree attracted two independent Section 13 exceptions, the court found it unnecessary to examine remaining objections regarding arbitration, forum shopping, and corporate liability. The exceptions are not cumulative,, the court noted, and even one was sufficient to render a foreign judgement inconclusive. Case background Latif Hakeem’s appeal arises from a commercial dispute between Samsung Gulf Electronics FZE and its former distributors, Greentalk FZE and Mobile Sales & Distribution (MS&D). Greentalk was initially Samsung’s sole distributor for Afghanistan, later expanding to Iran, while MS&D was set up in Pakistan at Samsung’s request. The appellants claim the tech company extended open-account credit, incentives, and price-support programmes, but by May 2020 owed them about $26.6 million in unpaid reimbursements. The tech company countered that the distributors owed roughly $21 million under credit facilities and has pursued civil and criminal actions in multiple jurisdictions. The distribution agreements dated January 1, 2020 included arbitration clauses under DIFC-LCIA Rules. However, Samsung filed cases in Dubai, Islamabad, and Lahore. Greentalk and MS&D initiated arbitration, claiming unpaid incentives and breach of contract. The arbitral award later found the tech company’s court proceedings violated the arbitration agreement and permanently restrained it from continuing them. Nevertheless, it obtained a money decree from the Dubai Court of First Instance, upheld by the Dubai Court of Cassation, and sought its enforcement in Pakistan. Published in Dawn, July 24th, 2026

items.title: Split among fuel dealers blunts impact of pump ‘shutdown’
items.description: • Association calls off strike for 15 days in late-night development after govt assurances • Most fuel stations remain open as rival group decides to stay away from protest LAHORE: A split among petroleum dealers blunted the impact of Thursday’s nationwide shutdown call, with petrol pumps remaining open in most urban and semi-urban areas across the country. However, late on Thursday night, the All Pakistan Petroleum Pump Owners Association (APPPOA) called off the shutdown for 15 days. In a video message, APPPOA Vice Chairman Noman Butt said the government had agreed to move a summary within 15 days for implementing the increase in dealers’ commission approved in December last year. It had also agreed to review the issue of daily fuel pricing within the same period. APPPOA Chairman Humayun Khan also announced an end to the shutdown call. In December, the Economic Coordination Committee (ECC) had approved an increase in dealers’ commission from Rs8.64 to Rs9.98 per litre in two phases by June 2026. However, the federal cabinet withheld its implementation, linking it to the complete digitalisation of fuel stocks and sales. The association’s rival group had already distanced itself from the shutdown call, significantly reducing its impact. Earlier in the day, a majority of pumps in Lahore, Faisalabad, Multan and other major cities of Punjab remained open, while Peshawar witnessed the closure of around 70 per cent of fuel stations. However, there were reports of pump closures in rural areas in various parts of the country and along GT Road. “All pump owners [nearly 450] in Lahore rejected the strike call given by one group of the petroleum dealers’ association. Therefore, they kept their pumps open as usual,” an owner of several petrol pumps in Lahore said. “There are many groups within the association. Everyone speaks his own words,” he added. Another pump owner said they rejected the strike call in view of their agreements with oil marketing companies (OMCs). “Our agreement binds us not to engage in such activities, as we are required to keep our pumps open round the clock under the agreement with our OMC,” he explained. In southern Punjab, district administrations reportedly foiled attempts by owners to close their petrol pumps. However, former petroleum dealers’ association president Zafar Mazari claimed that 90pc of petrol pumps from Karachi to Rahim Yar Khan remained closed on the association’s call. “I travelled from Karachi to Hyderabad on Thursday. I saw that 90pc of the pumps on GT Road were closed,” claimed Mazari, president of the Rahim Yar Khan District Petroleum Dealers Association. However, he admitted that the association was divided into two major groups, the Sami Khan group of Karachi and the Humayun Khan group of Khanewal. According to Pakistan Petroleum Dealers Association (PPDA) Chief Adviser Malik Khuda Bukhsh, almost all petrol pumps across the country remained open, barring some pumps in Peshawar owned by the rival group that wanted the shutdown. Bukhsh said Federal Minister for Energy Ali Pervaiz Malik had assured a PPDA delegation that the government would make every possible effort to address the genuine issues and valid demands of petroleum dealers within the next two weeks. Closure in Peshawar In Peshawar, more than 70pc of petrol pumps remained closed following the strike call. PPDA Khyber Pakhtunkhwa President Abdul Majid told Dawn that around 30pc of fuel stations in the provincial capital remained open. Majid said he kept his own fuel station open throughout the day, as did others affiliated with his association. Besides, he added that company-operated petrol pumps also remained open. The APPPOA had on Tuesday announced the closure of around 15,000 petrol pumps across the country from Wednesday night after the first round of negotiations with the government over daily petroleum pricing and dealers’ commission failed. Besides opposing daily fuel pricing, dealers are demanding an eight per cent commission, which now works out to about Rs25 per litre instead of the existing Rs8.64 per litre on both petrol and diesel. They are also seeking an end to supply quotas imposed by OMCs. Office-bearers of the association in some cities, including Faisalabad, claimed to have observed the strike. However, most petrol pumps in Toba Tek Singh and several other cities remained open. Most pumps in Sialkot, Narowal and other cities of upper Punjab also remained open on Thursday. Khaleeq Kiani from Islamabad, Manzoor Ali from Peshawar, Tariq Saeed from Toba Tek Singh, Malik Irfanul Haq from Rahim Yar Khan, Abid Mahmood from Narowal and Aamir Shafaat Khan from Karachi contributed to this report Published in Dawn, July 24th, 2026

items.title: Pakistan warns Houthi rebels to steer clear of its ships
items.description: • Says armed forces vigilant about situation in Bab el-Mandab; will give swift response if Islamabad’s assets come under attack • PM reassures Saudi crown prince of full support, condemns Yemeni rebels’ attacks on oil tankers • Dar meets Omani FM on sidelines of Asean moot; FO says efforts on to secure de-escalation ISLAMABAD: Pakistan on Thursday doubled down on its warning to Yemen’s Houthi rebels, describing any attack on its maritime interests as a “red line” and declaring that such a strike would be treated as an attack on the country, while simultaneously reassuring Saudi Arabia of its support and pledging to work with Riyadh to ensure the uninterrupted flow of ships through the Bab el-Mandab chokepoint. At his weekly media briefing, Foreign Office spokesperson Tahir Andrabi left little room for ambiguity about Islamabad’s position. “I do not think that there is a thin line when it comes to defending our national security and our assets. This is not a thin line; this is a red line. This red line is strictly within the context of our own national security,” he said, adding that any attack against Pakistani flagged vessels or commercial ships belonging to the country “will be treated as an attack against Pakistan and will be met with a swift response in the exercise of our right to self-defence’’. He said the determination of what constituted a threat or an attack would remain a technical assessment to be made by Pakistan’s armed forces, which would remain vigilant in the area. The spokesperson further reaffirmed that Islamabad remained fully committed to all bilateral agreements with Saudi Arabia, including a mutual defence arrangement that had been duly concluded and ratified. Pakistan had last year signed a defence pact with Saudi Arabia, according to which any attack against one country will be considered aggression against both. The remarks came hours after Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif spoke by telephone with Saudi Crown Prince and Prime Minister Mohammed Bin Salman, during which he condemned in the strongest terms the Houthi attacks on Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, describing them as unacceptable, a violation of international law and a threat to freedom of navigation, commercial shipping and regional peace and stability. The prime minister reiterated Pakistan’s unwavering support for the Kingdom’s security, sovereignty, territorial integrity and prosperity, and assured the crown prince that Islamabad would continue working closely with Saudi Arabia and the international community in support of peace, security and the uninterrupted flow of lawful maritime commerce through the Red Sea, the Strait of Hormuz and adjoining waters, according to a statement issued by PM Office. Pakistan’s latest warning follows FO’s condemnation a day earlier of Houthi threats against Saudi Arabia and commercial shipping after the group announced on July 20 an immediate maritime embargo against the Kingdom, portraying the move as retaliation for what it called the longstanding Saudi led siege of Houthi controlled ports and airports and a recent strike on Sana’a airport. De-escalation in Mideast The Foreign Office spokesperson also indicated that Pakistan remained committed to efforts aimed at de-escalating the conflict in the Gulf despite the setbacks suffered by the peace process. Andrabi said Islamabad had not abandoned hope that the two sides could eventually return to negotiations even as military operations continued. “Pakistan has been advocating for peace and the necessity of giving peace a chance through diplomacy and dialogue. We believe that the best template to revert to the peace process remains the Islamabad MoU and the Joint Statement between Pakistan and Qatar, which offers a roadmap for implementing this peace process,” he said. His remarks came as Pakistan continued consultations with key regional countries involved in mediation efforts. Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar met Omani Foreign Minister Sayyid Badr Al Busaidi on the sidelines of the Asean Regional Forum in Manila, where the two leaders reviewed the latest regional and international developments. According to FO, the two ministers expressed concern over the recent deterioration in the security situation, underscored the importance of preserving the ceasefire, respecting international law and working towards lasting peace and stability in the region. “We have not lost hope. We had not lost hope in the darkest days of this conflict cycle, when escalations mounted and there were armed attacks back and forth. Even then, we were not discouraged. We remain hopeful, however grim the situation may be on the ground. It is with the same degree of optimism that we will carry forward our advocacy for dialogue and diplomacy,” Andrabi added. GSP+ report and Amnesty claims The FO spokesperson, meanwhile, expressed disappointment with the European Union’s latest assessment of Generalised Scheme of Preferences implementation, saying the report did not present a sufficiently balanced picture of the country’s progress despite acknowledging legislative reforms and continued compliance with the program’s international conventions. “Nevertheless, I would be remiss if I did not express disappointment with the assessment, as the report’s overall narrative does not present a sufficiently balanced picture of Pakistan’s performance. It understates the breadth of reforms undertaken since Pakistan joined GSP Plus in 2014 and places disproportionate emphasis on the lower-than-desirable level of progress in certain areas,” Andrabi said. He reaffirmed that GSP+ remained central to Pakistan’s economic relationship with EU. The report had noted “issues” in Pakistan’s compliance with its GSP+ commitments and warned that Islamabad would have to address those shortcomings to qualify under the revised GSP framework, due to take effect from Jan 1, 2027. While the report acknowledged legislative progress, including measures relating to anti-torture rules, minority rights and labour standards, it said Pakistan had regressed in several areas, citing concerns over enforced disappearances, freedom of expression, judicial independence, minority rights and implementation of labour protections. The Foreign Office also rejected an Amnesty International report that called for investigation into Pakistan’s airstrike on Kabul in March, dismissing the allegations as unsubstantiated and urging the rights organisation and the wider international community to instead focus on what it described as the Afghan Taliban’s continued patronage of terrorist groups operating from Afghan territory. Andrabi said the allegations levelled by Amnesty lacked credible evidence. He further urged the international community to hold the Afghan Taliban accountable for failing to fulfil its obligations under relevant United Nations Security Council resolutions, the Doha Agreement and other commitments to prevent Afghan territory from being used for cross border terrorism. “Pakistan reserves the right to take all necessary measures to safeguard its sovereignty, territorial integrity, and the safety and security of its people,” he added. Syed Irfan Raza in Islamabad also contributed to this report Published in Dawn, July 24th, 2026

items.title: China, ASEAN FMs issue statement on Middle East, energy cooperation
items.description: China and ASEAN member states have issued a joint statement on addressing the regional impacts of developments in the Middle East and strengthening regional energy cooperation. The statement was released following the China-ASEAN Foreign Ministers' Meeting held on July 22 in Manila, the Philippines. In the statement, China and ASEAN noted that they have maintained communication and cooperation on international and regional issues of common interest and responses. The two sides recognized that the rapidly evolving situation in the Middle East and its spillover effects pose a grave threat to the lives and safety of civilians, as well as to regional and global peace, stability, and economic activities including regional trade, investment, energy and food security. The statement stressed the importance of resolving disputes through dialogue, diplomacy and peaceful means in accordance with international law. They also noted China's efforts in putting forth the four-point proposal on promoting peace and stability in the Middle East.

items.title: Ebola en RDC: la Première ministre en visite en Ituri, épicentre de l’épidémie
items.description: La Première ministre de la République Démocratique du Congo (RDC) est en visite dans les territoires touchés par l’épidémie d’Ebola. Judith Suminwa Tuluka est arrivée jeudi 23 juillet 2026 après-midi à Kisangani et doit se rendre à Bunia, Mongbwalu et Rwampara, pour « apprécier les réalités du terrain ». Pour Jean Bosco Kisoke, acteur de la société civile de l’Ituri, cette visite express, d’à peine un jour, ne suffit pas.

items.title: Petrol price up by Rs4.40, diesel by Rs3.62 per litre
items.description: Pakistan fuel prices, 2026 Official ex-depot prices (PKR/litre) — OGRA & Ministry of Energy notifications Petrol (MS-92) Diesel (HSD) Pre-crisis baseline Source: OGRA / Ministry of Energy (Petroleum Division); Dawn.com · "> The government on Thursday increased the prices of petrol and high-speed diesel (HSD) by Rs4.40 and Rs3.62 per litre, respectively, passing on the impact of fluctuating global oil prices following renewed hostilities in the Persian Gulf. Following the changes, the price of petrol stands at Rs331.52 per litre, while that of HSD stands at Rs378.66 per litre. The government is charging a total of Rs110 per litre in taxes and duties on petrol and Rs96 on diesel. The Petroleum Division’s notification said the new prices would be applicable on July 24 (Friday). The diesel price has come down from a peak of Rs520.35 recorded on April 3. Its price had started rising from Rs281 per litre after the US-Iran war broke out on February 28. The petrol price had peaked at Rs458.41 on April 3 after beginning its upward trajectory from Rs266 in the first week of March. Earlier, Petroleum Minister Ali Pervaiz Malik announced that fuel prices would now be fixed on a daily basis due to fluctuations in international market prices following renewed hostilities between Iran and the US. The government had been announcing weekly revisions to fuel prices since early March, alongside measures for the conservation of fuel amid possible oil supply disruptions due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East. The federal government in April also announced targeted relief measures to provide subsidised fuel. The petroleum minister stated that the cabinet and the prime minister had decided to give the Oil and Gas Regulatory Authority (Ogra) the responsibility of deciding fuel prices on a daily basis based on international market trends. The daily pricing decision was rejected by the All Pakistan Dealers Association, which said it would consider a protest plan this week. Petrol is mainly used in private transport, small vehicles, rickshaws and two-wheelers, and changes in its price affect the middle and lower-middle classes. Similarly, changes in diesel prices also impact the public at large, as it is mainly used in the heavy transport sector, power plants and large generators. Petrol and high-speed diesel (HSD) are the major revenue earners, with monthly sales of about 700,000 to 800,000 tonnes, compared to just 10,000 tonnes of monthly demand for kerosene.

items.title: PGLR+ : plus de 10 250 jeunes leaders formés et accompagnés
items.description: © JDM La 2e phase du Programme de gouvernance locale redevable (PGLR+) s’est achevée en juin dernier, après cinq années de mise en œuvre. Sa cérémonie de clôture s’est tenue le 23 juillet 2026 à Bamako, pour dresser le bilan et réfléchir à la pérennisation des acquis. Organisée par l’Organisation néerlandaise de développement, SNV, en partenariat avec le Conseil national de la jeunesse du Mali (CNJ-Mali) et sous la présidence du ministère de la Jeunesse et des Sports, chargé de l’Instruction civique et de la Construction citoyenne, la rencontre a placé l’après-programme au cœur des échanges. Institutions, collectivités et organisations de jeunesse ont notamment discuté des moyens de prolonger les dynamiques engagées. « Cette deuxième phase a commencé en 2021 dans un contexte un peu particulier, mais a permis de nombreuses avancées en matière d’engagement des jeunes dans les mécanismes de prise de décision », a souligné Freddy Sahinguvu, coordinateur national du PGLR+. Mis en œuvre d’avril 2021 à juin 2026 grâce à un financement de 21 millions d’euros des Pays-Bas, le programme a couvert 240 communes dans huit régions du centre et du nord du Mali, notamment Ségou, San, Mopti, Bandiagara, Douentza, Tombouctou, Gao et Ménaka, plus trois communes pilotes à Bougouni. Conduite par un consortium réunissant SNV, Oxfam, la Fondation Voice4Thought (V4T), CORDAID et neuf partenaires locaux, cette phase prolonge un processus engagé en 2014. Des jeunes davantage présents dans les instances locales Le bilan fait état de plus de 10 250 jeunes leaders formés et accompagnés, dont 4 983 jeunes femmes. Plus de 3 700 organisations de jeunesse ont aussi été soutenues. Dans le même temps, de jeunes leaders occupent désormais 10 390 postes de responsabilité au sein des structures locales. Le PGLR+ a soutenu 1 469 cadres de concertation entre citoyens et autorités. Dans 76% des communes partenaires, des débats publics sont désormais organisés autour du budget primitif, offrant aux populations un droit de regard accru sur les choix communaux. S’y ajoutent 7 362 actions citoyennes en faveur des services publics et 2 257 opportunités économiques créées pour les jeunes. Le taux de confiance entre citoyens et autorités locales a atteint 68,7%, dépassant la cible initiale. La proportion de points d’eau fonctionnels s’est établie à 83,8%, tandis que plus des trois quarts des conflits liés aux ressources naturelles soumis aux mécanismes locaux ont été prévenus ou réglés pacifiquement. L’autonomisation des femmes constitue un autre acquis majeur. Au total, 583 jeunes femmes ont bénéficié de plus de 27 000 séances de mentorat assurées par 139 mentors. Plus de 72% d’entre elles occupent désormais une responsabilité dans une instance de décision. Parallèlement, 73 associations villageoises d’épargne et de crédit, regroupant près de 1 750 membres, ont été accompagnées : plus de 92% des adhérentes ont accru leurs revenus et plus de 91% ont développé une activité génératrice de revenus. Le défi de la pérennisation La clôture a aussi permis de confronter ces résultats aux conclusions de l’évaluation finale et aux expériences des bénéficiaires. Les échanges ont mis en évidence la nécessité de préserver les compétences et les mécanismes locaux construits durant la mise en œuvre. Pour Sory Ibrahim Cissé, président du CNJ-Mali, cet héritage constitue la principale force du programme. « Si le PGLR+ arrive à son terme, son expertise, ses méthodes et ses compétences continueront de vivre à travers les jeunes, les communautés et les leaders du CNJ. Les financements prennent fin, mais les connaissances et les capacités restent. C’est là le plus grand héritage de ce programme. » Plusieurs acquis pourraient servir de points d’appui : l’institutionnalisation progressive du Concours de transparence des communes et des organes de gestion des services publics, l’intégration envisagée du mentorat aux politiques publiques et le renforcement de 129 Maisons des jeunes. Le PGLR+ laisse également 58 études réalisées par de jeunes chercheurs sur la gouvernance participative, les conflits, l’inclusion sociale et les normes de genre. Le ministre Abdoul Kassim Ibrahim Fomba a assuré que l’État entendait prendre le relais. « Nous sommes à une phase de clôture, mais ce n’est pas la fin. Nous allons prendre le relais au niveau de l’État. Nous avons besoin des jeunes formés pour qu’ils continuent à transmettre ce qu’ils ont appris à d’autres jeunes », a-t-il déclaré. Mohamed Kenouvi

items.title: Alerte rouge sur l’or noir : Le prix du baril monte au dessus de 100 dollars et fait vaciller l’économie mondiale
items.description: Le monde financier retient son souffle. Selon les informations rapportées par le dossier « Le pétrole repasse au-dessus de 100 dollars, les Bourses plongent », le prix du baril de Brent a franchi ce jeudi la barre symbolique des 100 dollars pour la première fois depuis mai dernier. Ce rebond brutal, provoqué par l'intensification du conflit au Moyen-Orient avec l'attaque de pétroliers saoudiens en mer Rouge, a immédiatement déclenché une onde de choc sur les places boursières mondiales. Entre la dégringolade des valeurs technologiques et l'envolée des taux d'intérêt à des niveaux inédits depuis 2009 pour certains pays, l'économie globale fait face au spectre d'un nouveau cycle inflationniste dévastateur. La mer Rouge est-elle en train de devenir le talon d'Achille de la croissance mondiale ? L'attaque revendiquée par les rebelles houthis contre deux pétroliers saoudiens a mis le feu aux poudres sur le marché des matières premières. En quelques heures, le baril de Brent a bondi de près de 7%, s’établissant à 100,52 dollars, tandis que son équivalent américain, le WTI, grimpait au-dessus de 92 dollars. L'enjeu n'est pas seulement pétrolier, il est logistique. Comme le souligne l'expert Stephen Innes dans les colonnes de notre source, la mer Rouge permet aux exportations saoudiennes de contourner le détroit d'Ormuz via le port de Yanbu. En frappant cet axe stratégique, les rebelles ont brisé l'hypothèse d'une crise géographiquement limitée, forçant les investisseurs à revoir leurs scénarios de risque. L'effet domino ne s'est pas fait attendre. Les places européennes ont terminé la journée dans le rouge vif : Milan a chuté de 2,80%, Paris a cédé 1,64% et Madrid 1,55%. Ce repli est accentué par la débâcle du secteur technologique. Aux États-Unis, le Nasdaq a plongé de 2,55%, emporté par les plongeons spectaculaires de Tesla (-14,24%) et d’Alphabet (-7,12%) après des résultats trimestriels décevants. En Europe, le fleuron des semi-conducteurs STMicroelectronics a connu un véritable naufrage boursier, s’effondrant de 17,72% à Paris. Au-delà des cours de Bourse, c'est le coût de la vie et de la dette qui inquiète. La remontée des prix de l'énergie alimente les craintes d'une inflation persistante. En conséquence, les taux d’emprunt des États s'envolent. Pour la première fois depuis juin 2009, les taux d’emprunt de la France à 10 ans ont dépassé les 4%. Même constat en Allemagne et aux États-Unis, où les rendements atteignent des sommets. Malgré ce contexte électrique, la Banque centrale européenne (BCE) a choisi la prudence en laissant ses taux directeurs inchangés ce jeudi. Mais pour combien de temps ? Si l'or noir maintient son cap au-dessus des 100 dollars, les banques centrales pourraient être contraintes de durcir à nouveau le ton, au risque de freiner un peu plus une économie mondiale déjà chancelante. L’article Alerte rouge sur l'or noir : Le prix du baril monte au dessus de 100 dollars et fait vaciller l’économie mondiale est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Chinese Vice Foreign Minister Ma Zhaoxu visits the United States
items.description: Chinese Vice Foreign Minister Ma Zhaoxu visited the United States from July 22 to 23, at the invitation of the US side, the Chinese Foreign Ministry said on Friday. During the visit, Ma held consultations with US Deputy Secretary of State Christopher Landau, met with Ivan Kanapathy, senior director for Asia at the White House's National Security Council, and US Under Secretary of War for Policy Elbridge Colby, and exchanged views with members of both the US Senate and House of Representatives. According to the statement, the two sides had in-depth exchanges of views on implementing the important common understandings reached by the two heads of state and promoting the development of a constructive strategic stability relationship between China and the United States.

items.title: Iran ramps up executions under shadow of war
items.description: Vahid Baniamerian's family only found out he was going to be executed when Iranian state media reports flashed on their phones, saying the supreme court would review his death sentence. "They did not give any warning. Even the lawyer didn't know," a close relative of Baniamerian, based outside of Iran, told AFP, asking not to be identified. "It was completely illegal, even based on their own oppressive laws."

items.title: Chinese foreign minister meets Belarusian counterpart
items.description: Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi met with Belarusian Foreign Minister Maxim Ryzhenkov on Thursday ahead of the Meeting of the Council of the Ministers of Foreign Affairs of the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO) Member States in Kyrgyzstan. Wang, also a member of the Political Bureau of the Communist Party of China Central Committee, said China and Belarus are trustworthy partners. Under the strategic guidance of their presidents, the China-Belarus all-weather comprehensive strategic partnership has maintained strong momentum in recent years, with fruitful cooperation achieved across various fields, he added. Next year marks the 35th anniversary of the establishment of diplomatic ties between China and Belarus, Wang said. Standing at a new historical starting point, China is ready to work with Belarus to follow the direction charted by the two heads of state, strengthen exchanges at all levels, enhance the alignment of development strategies, deepen exchanges on governance experience, promote the high-quality development of bilateral relations, advance all-round cooperation, and deliver greater benefits to the two peoples, he added. Wang said mutual support is an essential feature of the China-Belarus all-weather comprehensive strategic partnership. He added that China will, as always, support Belarus in safeguarding its sovereignty, independence and territorial integrity, pursuing a development path suited to its national conditions, and oppose external interference in Belarus's internal affairs under any pretext. He said Belarus has consistently stood firmly with China on issues concerning China's core interests, which China highly appreciates. Wang congratulated Belarus on becoming a founding member of the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization. He said China looks forward to strengthening coordination with Belarus within the SCO framework, advancing the establishment of the "four centers" in the security field and the preparation for the SCO Development Bank, and promoting the SCO's entry into a new stage of high-quality development. Ryzhenkov said the two heads of state have maintained close, high-level exchanges, leading bilateral relations to an unprecedented level and forging ironclad, brotherly mutual trust. He thanked China for its long-standing support and assistance, saying Belarus will remain firmly committed to developing relations with China, advancing comprehensive cooperation and drawing on China's successful experience. He said Belarus looks forward to taking the 35th anniversary of diplomatic ties next year as an opportunity to strengthen cooperation with China in such areas as industry, investment and information technology, while expanding subnational and people-to-people exchanges. Belarus is honored to have joined the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization and is willing to maintain close communication and coordination with China on multilateral platforms including the SCO, Ryzhenkov said. Facts have proven that Belarus has chosen the right path, and the two countries enjoy even broader prospects for future cooperation, he added. The two sides also signed the 2026-2027 Consultation Plan between the Foreign Ministries of China and Belarus.

items.title: Chinese foreign minister meets Uzbek counterpart
items.description: Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi met with Uzbek Foreign Minister Bakhtiyor Saidov on Thursday evening ahead of the Meeting of the Council of the Ministers of Foreign Affairs of the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO) Member States in Kyrgyzstan. Wang, also a member of the Political Bureau of the Communist Party of China (CPC) Central Committee, said this year marks the 10th anniversary of the establishment of the comprehensive strategic partnership between China and Uzbekistan, a year that builds on past achievements and opens up a new future. Wang said that strategic mutual trust has continued to deepen and practical cooperation has expanded across all fields over the past decade, with bilateral relations achieving a historic leap from a comprehensive strategic partnership to an all-weather comprehensive strategic partnership for a new era. Noting that this year marks the 35th anniversary of Uzbekistan's independence, Wang expressed confidence in Uzbekistan's development under the leadership of President Shavkat Mirziyoyev, saying China will firmly support Uzbekistan in pursuing a development path suited to its national conditions and believes Uzbekistan will continue to firmly support China on issues concerning China's core interests. China is ready to work with Uzbekistan to fully unlock the potential for cooperation, promote the high-quality construction of the China-Kyrgyzstan-Uzbekistan railway, and create more landmark projects in new industrialization, Wang said. Noting that China initiated the establishment of the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization to help developing countries strengthen AI capacity building and advance their modernization, Wang congratulated Uzbekistan on becoming a founding member of the organization. He also said that both sides should make good use of the China-Uzbekistan law enforcement and security cooperation mechanism to combat the "three evil forces" of terrorism, extremism and separatism, as well as transnational organized crime, and strengthen coordination on multilateral platforms to promote a more just and equitable global governance system. Saidov said Uzbekistan cherishes its brotherly friendship with China, and developing relations with China remains a priority of its foreign policy. He extended warm congratulations on the 105th anniversary of the founding of the CPC, saying that, from its founding, the CPC has upheld the principle of serving the people wholeheartedly and has led the Chinese people to tremendous achievements. Uzbekistan is ready to learn from China's experience in governance, align its "Uzbekistan-2030" strategy with the Belt and Road Initiative, and accelerate its own development and revitalization. Saidov said Uzbekistan firmly adheres to the one-China principle, opposes any actions that undermine China's sovereignty and territorial integrity, and stands ready to strengthen exchanges at all levels with China, deepen practical cooperation in trade, investment, energy, critical minerals and the digital economy, jointly combat the "three evil forces," and tap the potential for subnational cooperation. He said that Uzbekistan supports the vision of building a community with a shared future for humanity and the four global initiatives proposed by China. Uzbekistan is pleased to join the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization and is willing to continue strengthening communication and coordination with China on multilateral platforms, promote the development of the SCO and the China-Central Asia mechanism, and jointly uphold multilateralism.

items.title: Burkina Faso: RSF accuse Ibrahim Traoré de posséder une «milice numérique» qui cible les journalistes
items.description: Au Burkina Faso, Reporters Sans Frontières (RSF), accuse Ibrahim Traoré d'entretenir « une milice numérique » qui cible les journalistes. Dans une enquête publiée le 23 juillet 2026, l'ONG soutient que ces cyberactivistes, rattachés à la junte et connus sous l'appellation de Bataillons d'intervention rapide ( BIr-C), « occupent le cyberespace et s'attaquent aux voix dissidentes en toute impunité ».

items.title: Burkina Faso : Le gouvernement consacre près de 38 milliards de FCFA à la construction de deux lycées professionnels spécialisés et de 17 complexes scolaires, préscolaires et primaires
items.description: Le gouvernement burkinabè franchit une étape décisive dans sa stratégie de développement endogène. Réuni en séance ordinaire ce jeudi 23 juillet 2026 sous la présidence du Capitaine Ibrahim Traoré, le Conseil des ministres a entériné des décisions structurantes pour l’avenir de la jeunesse et de l'industrie nationale. Au cœur des délibérations : l’opérationnalisation de l’Académie technologique du Faso (ATF) et le lancement de projets de construction d’envergure portés par le Bureau national des Grands projets du Burkina. Avec une enveloppe globale de près de 38 milliards de F CFA financée sur fonds propres, le Burkina Faso réaffirme sa volonté de transformer ses ressources locales par une formation technique d’excellence. Le Conseil des ministres tenu ce 23 juillet 2026 marquera sans doute un tournant dans la politique industrielle et éducative du pays. Pendant plus de cinq heures de débats, les membres du gouvernement ont adopté deux rapports majeurs qui lient directement la formation académique aux besoins réels de l’économie nationale. Le premier acte fort de ce Conseil a été l’approbation des statuts de l’Académie technologique du Faso (ATF). Ce projet de décret n’est pas qu’une simple formalité administrative ; il constitue l’acte de naissance opérationnel de cette institution. En la dotant d’un cadre règlementaire clair, les autorités entendent faire de l’ATF un moteur de l’innovation technologique nationale, capable de répondre aux défis de la modernisation du pays. Le second volet des délibérations concerne l'offre éducative concrète. Le Conseil a donné son feu vert à une série de projets spécifiques sous l'égide du Bureau national des Grands projets du Burkina (BN-GPB). L'ambition est vaste : la construction de deux lycées professionnels spécialisés ainsi que de 17 complexes scolaires, préscolaires et primaires. L’objectif affiché par le gouvernement est sans équivoque : il s’agit de former des ressources humaines hautement qualifiées pour assurer la transformation des matières premières sur place. Cette stratégie vise à rompre avec l’exportation de ressources brutes pour privilégier la création de valeur ajoutée au niveau national. Le coût total de ces infrastructures s’élève à trente-sept milliards neuf cent cinquante un millions trois cent quinze mille six cent quatre-vingt-six (37 951 315 686) F CFA TTC. Dans un contexte de recherche permanente de souveraineté, le gouvernement a précisé que ce financement est intégralement assuré par le Budget de l’Etat, exercice 2026. À travers ces investissements massifs, le Burkina Faso de l'ère du Capitaine Ibrahim Traoré mise sur le binôme « Technologie et Formation » pour transformer durablement le visage économique du pays. La pose de la première pierre de ces futurs fleurons de l'éducation professionnelle est désormais attendue par une jeunesse impatiente de prendre part à la transformation industrielle de la patrie. items.title: The Art of the Giveaway
items.description: W hat kind of negotiator gives the other side everything that it wants with the hope of eventually getting something in return? It’s a question that even some of President Trump’s own allies are asking after his administration signed a landmark deal to develop a civilian nuclear program in Saudi Arabia using American technology. The deal’s opponents, in the United States and Israel, worry that the new pact might ultimately allow for uranium enrichment—and if so, that Trump just gave Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman the tools he needs to build a bomb. What did the U.S. require in return? Pretty much zip. That, at least, is how things stood when the agreement was finalized yesterday. Then, just before 8 a.m. today, Trump took to social media to deliver what seemed like an after-the-fact ultimatum: The deal, he wrote, is “totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords,” the Middle East initiative launched in Trump’s first term that is designed to normalize relations between Israel and countries in the region. Trump-administration officials argued that the deal is an “America First” policy triumph because the 30-year, multibillion-dollar agreement gives American companies (which already do huge business in Saudi Arabia) a central role in developing the kingdom’s nuclear infrastructure while shutting out foreign competitors. Karoline Leavitt, the White House press secretary, told reporters today that the arrangement “will ultimately benefit American industry workers and supply chains.” It may not benefit Trump’s image as a consummate dealmaker, however. The framework of Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham accords and, in return, getting defense guarantees and U.S. help to develop a civilian nuclear program has been on the table for years. It came close to fruition during the Biden administration. But the kingdom received a defense package in November and the nuclear deal this week. Neither agreement stipulated the normalizing of relations with Israel as a precondition. “Leave it to this administration to negotiate after the signatures are barely dry,” Elisa Ewers, a senior fellow for Middle East studies at the Council on Foreign Relations, told us. When the agreement was unveiled yesterday, U.S. lawmakers and Israeli politicians were startled by its lax conditions for Riyadh. Trump by this morning may have decided to provide reassurance with his Abraham Accords announcement—so he did what he often does. “The president wakes up every day, and every day, at least on Middle East policy, is an improvisation,” F. Gregory Gause, a professor emeritus at the Bush School of Government and Public Service at Texas A&M University, told us. “He was getting some pushback on the Saudi deal, and so he added this sweetener to it for people in Congress who might feel that if the Saudis had a regular diplomatic relationship with Israel, they would be less concerned.” Trump, he noted, may improvise again and remove the stipulation if the furor dies down. Last week, the president said that the U.S. would impose a 20 percent fee on the value of the cargo on ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz to counter Iranian efforts to control the waterway. The next day, the president reversed himself. “So it could be that by tomorrow, this goes away from the president’s mind,” Gause said. But if he gives the Saudis what they want without asking for anything in return, he also would have thrown away all of his leverage. I n 2023, top officials in the Biden administration shuttled between Washington and Riyadh, believing that they were on the precipice of a major breakthrough: Bin Salman, known as MBS, was close to joining the Abraham Accords, a development that officials saw as a bulwark against Iran’s rising nuclear threat in the region. Talks to flesh out the details were scheduled for mid-October of that year. But the negotiations unraveled after Hamas’s October 7 attack on Israel and the devastating Gaza war that followed. In the aftermath, Saudi leaders concluded that normalization was politically untenable. Biden-administration officials then looked for other ways to entice the Saudis to both restart normalization talks and help secure the Gaza Strip. They focused on a mutual-defense agreement, which included nuclear cooperation, even though President Biden had made known his dislike for the crown prince after the 2018 murder of the journalist Jamal Khashoggi. Jon Wolfsthal, a former adviser on U.S.-Saudi nuclear cooperation, told us that the Biden administration had calculated that “if the U.S. didn’t get in the game, particularly as a nuclear provider, one of America’s adversaries might, and that is much worse for U.S. national security.” But with the 2024 election looming, Saudi Arabia’s government gambled that a Trump victory would bring more favorable terms, and the talks stalled. [ Read: Iraq’s man in the middle ] The Saudis were right. Saudi leaders already saw in Trump a partner whose approach was similar to their own: pragmatic, transactional, and largely unbothered by concerns about human rights. The relationship extended beyond official diplomacy. MBS had developed a close rapport with Trump’s son-in-law Jared Kushner during Trump’s first term, communicating with him directly over WhatsApp. Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund had previously committed $2 billion to Kushner’s private-equity firm. Following his return to the Oval Office, Trump grew fixated on getting Saudi Arabia to normalize ties with Israel. As in his first term, Trump picked Saudi Arabia for his first official overseas visit, arriving with an entourage of American business executives in an effort to foster deeper corporate ties, particularly in technology. During a meeting in Washington, D.C., in November, Trump, raising his voice, pressed MBS to join the Abraham Accords. MBS insisted that the only way to achieve this would be by agreeing on a path toward Palestinian statehood. (Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has rejected the prospect of a sovereign Palestinian state.) Still, the U.S. and Saudi Arabia reached an understanding on a defense agreement, which would include the sale of advanced F-35 fighter jets. Months later, Trump tried to link an expansion of the Abraham Accords to his plan for peace with Iran, suggesting in a conference call with several regional leaders that they sign up, according to U.S. and Middle Eastern officials. After that conversation finished, he called MBS personally to press the issue once more. But Trump got the same answer. Still, talks over a Saudi civilian nuclear program continued. T he Trump administration says that this week’s deal is designed to help Saudi Arabia develop civilian nuclear power only. Energy Secretary Chris Wright, who signed the agreement, said in a statement that the terms “uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation.” The Saudi government said that the deal supports its domestic efforts to diversify energy sources, and that the government is committed to the “highest international standards” of nuclear safety, security, and nonproliferation. But Saudi Arabia has also talked about becoming a nuclear-weapons power if Iran were able to develop its own weapon. Preventing Tehran from doing so is a central goal of the U.S. conflict, but its outcome is uncertain, as is the fate of Iran’s nuclear program. “Saudi Arabia does not want to acquire any nuclear bomb, but without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible,” MBS told CBS News in 2018. Advocates of nonproliferation contend that the U.S.-Saudi deal lacks the necessary guardrails to prevent that: no measures to prevent the sharing of uranium-enrichment and plutonium-separation technologies, no commitment by Saudi Arabia to comply with the safety standards of, and monitoring by, the International Atomic Energy Agency. Under the agreement, American companies would build nuclear reactors in Saudi Arabia, and the two countries would study whether Saudi Arabia should eventually enrich uranium on its own instead of importing nuclear fuel. Enriching uranium is necessary to produce the fuel for nuclear-power plants but can also be a key step toward making a nuclear weapon. “Your best route to avoiding proliferation is this kind of very, very close relationship with the United States, with U.S. technology, that we control, that we monitor, and that is of a long duration,” Victoria Coates, a former deputy national security adviser to Trump, who now works at the Heritage Foundation, told us. Trump denied that enrichment is on the table. The deal notes that the U.S. will ultimately decide, after a study is conducted, whether enrichment shouldn’t take place, and if that’s the conclusion, Saudi Arabia would be barred from pursuing it on its own or with another country for 10 years. But giving the U.S., rather than international inspectors, that authority could lead other regimes to choose their own inspectors too, opening the door to broader proliferation. [ Read: The forever negotiation ] “Making all of this subjective—and ‘you can choose your own inspectors,’ and all those other things—is a really bad route to go down,” Jon Alterman, a global-security scholar at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, told us. In theory, he said, the agreement could give Iranians the license and precedent to credibly say: “We have Russian inspectors, we have Chinese inspectors, so we’re doing just what the Saudis are doing.” Alterman noted that the U.S. even has a model for a better arrangement: a 2009 agreement with the United Arab Emirates. “The Emiratis built a genuinely robust and safe domestic nuclear program,” Alterman said. “There are robust safeguards against proliferation. And it deepened the partnership between the UAE and the United States.” But that’s not what the U.S. asked of Saudi Arabia this week.

items.title: ONU: l'ex-président sénégalais Macky Sall défend sa candidature au poste de secrétaire général
items.description: À une semaine de l'ouverture officielle du processus de désignation du prochain secrétaire général des Nations unies, les six candidats ont présenté jeudi 23 juillet leur vision devant l'Assemblée générale à New York. Seul Africain en lice, l'ancien président sénégalais Macky Sall a défendu son expérience et son projet de réforme de l'organisation. Antonio Guterres quittera ses fonctions le 1ᵉʳ janvier 2027, au terme de deux mandats.

items.title: Crise du poisson à Ouaga : Les explications sur l’augmentation des prix jugées « insuffisantes »
items.description: Face à l’envolée soudaine des prix du poisson sur les étals de la capitale, l’autorité de régulation passe à l’offensive. Selon les informations rapportées dans l’article « Marché halieutique : la BMCRF en ligne de mire » , les équipes de la Brigade Mobile de Contrôle Économique et de la Répression des Fraudes (BMCRF) ont mené, ce 23 juillet 2026 , des visites inopinées auprès des importateurs et grossistes du marché halieutique. Cette opération coup de poing vise à faire la lumière sur les causes d'une pénurie qui fragilise le pouvoir d'achat des ménages burkinabè. Entre arguments liés à la crise mondiale et soupçons de spéculation, la « police économique » promet de poursuivre ses investigations jusqu'au rétablissement de prix justes. Le poisson, protéine de base dans de nombreux foyers burkinabè, est-il en train de devenir un produit de luxe ? C’est la question qui anime les marchés de Ouagadougou alors que les consommateurs constatent une brusque flambée des prix . Pour répondre à cette inquiétude, la BMCRF a investi le terrain ce jeudi pour échanger avec les acteurs majeurs de la filière. Lors de ces échanges, les importateurs et grossistes ont tenté de justifier cette situation par une conjoncture internationale défavorable. Plusieurs arguments techniques ont été avancés : L’évolution des modes de consommation : Dans les pays producteurs, les populations délaissent le poisson frais pour le poisson surgelé , réduisant mécaniquement les volumes destinés à l’exportation vers des pays comme le Burkina Faso. La baisse des rendements : Les pêcheurs signalent passer de plus longues heures en mer pour des captures de plus en plus maigres. L'épuisement des ressources : La diminution des espèces halieutiques est désormais décrite comme une crise mondiale , certaines espèces étant menacées de disparition. Cependant, ces explications n’ont pas totalement convaincu les agents de la BMCRF. Jugeant ces éléments « insuffisants » pour expliquer l'ampleur de la hausse actuelle, les enquêteurs ont décidé de ne pas en rester là. La Brigade a annoncé la poursuite de ses investigations pour lever le voile sur les mécanismes réels de cette augmentation et s'assurer qu'aucune rétention de stock ou spéculation illicite ne vient aggraver la situation. Au-delà du simple constat, la BMCRF a lancé un appel ferme à la collaboration des opérateurs économiques . L'objectif est clair : préserver le pouvoir d'achat des citoyens et garantir que le panier de la ménagère reste « accessible et équilibré » malgré les tensions internationales. L'institution rappelle aux consommateurs qu'ils peuvent signaler toute pratique de prix abusive en contactant les numéros verts mis à leur disposition (80001184 / 80001186). Le bras de fer pour la stabilisation du marché halieutique est désormais engagé. L’article Crise du poisson à Ouaga : Les explications sur l'augmentation des prix jugées « insuffisantes » est apparu en premier sur NetAfrique.net .

items.title: Séjour de la présidente de l’Union interparlementaire : Tulia Ackson découvre Laongo et le savoir-faire des artisanes
items.description: En séjour au Burkina Faso, la Présidente de l’Union interparlementaire (UIP), Tulia Ackson a visité, le jeudi 23 juillet 2026, le site de sculpture sur granite de Laongo dans la région de Oubri. Plus tard dans l’après-midi, elle est allée encourager les femmes de l’Unité artisanale de production Godé (UAP Godé) dans le quartier Kamsonghin de Ouagadougou. La Présidente de l’Union interparlementaire (UIP), Tulia Ackson est en visite officielle au Burkina Faso du 19 au 25 juillet 2026. Hier jeudi 23 juillet, elle a mis à profit son séjour pour se rendre dans la région de Oubri sur le site de sculpture sur granite de Laongo. Elle a été accueillie sur les lieux par le Président de l’Assemblée législative du peuple (ALT), Ousmane Bougouma, accompagné du secrétaire général du ministère en charge du tourisme, Fidèle Tamini. A l’issue de la visite guidée, le Président Ousmane Bougouma a rappelé que ce site est un patrimoine culturel exceptionnel du Burkina. « Nous sommes venus dans le cadre de la quatrième édition de la grande saison du tourisme interne. Cette année, cette grande édition porte sur le thème de la valorisation du patrimoine culturel et du développement local. Mme la Présidente de l’UIP étant en visite d’amitié et de travail, nous avons jugé bon de la faire découvrir un pan important de notre patrimoine culturel », a-t-il justifié. Le Burkina est une destination à ne pas manquer Il s’agit, selon lui, de montrer que le pays dispose d’un riche patrimoine culturel qu’il faut valoriser et surtout dire que le Burkina est une destination à ne pas manquer. « L’ALP en venant ici, a pour objectif de soutenir le ministère en charge du tourisme dans le cadre de la valorisation du patrimoine culturel, du développement de l’économie touristique », a-t-il rassuré. Il a enfin invité les Burkinabè à découvrir le patrimoine culturel en allant sur les sites touristiques. « Parce qu’on ne peut être patriote que lorsque l’on connaît véritablement son propre pays. Et notre pays s’exprime à travers sa culture, à travers son art », a-t-il estimé. Et, la Présidente de l’UIP d’apprécier la richesse que le site de Laongo montre en termes de l’histoire du Burkina. « On nous a raconté à travers les œuvres, comment les gens vivaient, comment les femmes étaient traitées, comment les femmes sont traitées aujourd’hui, et l’avenir de ces femmes », a-t-elle laissé entendre. Dans l’après-midi, la Présidente de l’UIP, Tulia Ackson s’est rendue dans le quartier Kamsonghin à Ouagadougou pour encourager les femmes qui travaillent dans l’unité artisanale de production Godé (UAP Godé) créé en 1987 par Thomas Sankara. Elle œuvre dans la production et la commercialisation du Faso Dan Fani. L’unité est sous la tutelle du ministère de la Famille et de la Solidarité. Selon sa responsable, Patricia Traoré, le centre participe à améliorer les conditions de vie des femmes qui y travaillent. Elle a traduit ses remerciements à la présidente pour le choix porté sur le centre. « Nous sommes honorés et fières de cette visite », a-t-elle dit. C’est également une présidente de l’UIP qui est sortie impressionner du centre. « C’est un centre qui aide les femmes à se rendre utiles pour leur pays, mais aussi pour elles-mêmes et leurs familles. Même avec des enfants au dos, certaines femmes montrent qu’elles peuvent travailler », a-t-elle apprécié. Adama SEDGO

items.title: China’s Police Harass Animal Protection Supporters
items.description: Click to expand Image A pedestrian walks a dog in Chongqing, China, on December 17, 2024. © 2024 Cheng Xin/Getty Images Two incidents of videoed cruelty of stray animals in China this summer have triggered widespread outrage. Both spread rapidly online, and both showed a familiar government response: censorship, intimidation, and dispersion. In late June, videos of four boys cruelly mistreating and burning a dog and her puppies in Jieyang, Guangdong province, circulated widely online. Public outrage was swift and overwhelming: paid billboards and other advertisements appeared in over 100 cities across the country called for an end to animal abuse. More than 70,000 people signed an online petitiondemanding accountability and a national animal protection law. Animal protection supporters abroad crowdfunded a billboard in Hong Kong’s Mong Kok district and purchased screen advertisements in Taipei and New York’s Times Square. On June 30, the government, while acknowledging that the abuse occurred and explaining the consequences for those responsible, also urged "netizens not to spread related information." Suppression followed. Social media platforms removed posts about “Wang Wang,” a generic and endearing term supporters used for the puppies’ mother. The Chinese social media site Weibo also reportedly blocked a celebrity’s statement and a related trending topic. Online, people have reported that police ordered memorial advertisements taken down from public screens in several cities. An organizer told the BBC that police had questioned and threatened dozens of others, telling them to stop their activism. The Jieyang response follows a pattern. Earlier in June, police in the municipality of Chongqing forcibly dispersed hundreds of residents who had gathered for days to protest another reported case of dog abuse. They injured several people and briefly took dozens away before detaining—under public pressure—the alleged abuser. Animal abuse has clearly touched a nerve in China, especially among young people for whom pets have become an important source of companionship. Peaceful expressions of public outrage should never be considered a threat to state security. Chinese authorities should stop suppressing public discussion of animal abuse, free any protesters still detained, and investigate police violence in Chongqing. They should also listen to what people are saying, whether it’s about personal freedom, the rights of the public, or the protection of Wang Wangs.

items.title: IVes championnats d’athlétisme U18 et U20 des cinq nations : le Burkina entend confirmer son leadership à domicile
items.description: La IVe édition des championnats d’athlétisme U18 et U20 des cinq nations s’annonce particulièrement relevée, mais les Burkinabè se sont préparés en conséquence afin de réaffirmer leur domination sur la compétition. L’information ainsi que d’autres détails de l’événement ont été portés à la connaissance des journalistes par les organisateurs lors d’une conférence de presse, le jeudi 23 juillet 2026, à Ouagadougou. Ils seront environ 200 athlètes et encadreurs du Bénin, de la Côte d’Ivoire, du Ghana, du Togo et du Burkina Faso à se mesurer à la faveur des IVes championnats d’athlétisme U18 et U20 des cinq nations. Prévus pour se tenir dans la capitale burkinabè les 25 et 26 juillet 2026, l’événement va concerner 32 épreuves au total soit 16 épreuves masculines et 16 épreuves féminines, a fait savoir le 1er vice-président de la fédération burkinabè d’athlétisme Missiri Sawadogo. Toutes les épreuves en athlétisme sont prises en compte à l’exception du lancer de marteau et de la perche. Cette compétition se tient à une période charnière de l’année en ce sens qu’elle constitue un tremplin pour la préparation des athlètes aux échéances internationales majeures des catégories concernées, a souligné les conférenciers. Il s’agit notamment des championnats du monde juniors des U20 à Eugène aux USA prévus pour le mois d’août et les jeux olympiques de la jeunesse à Dakar en octobre-novembre. « Ce qui fait que tous les pays y compris le Burkina ont engagé leurs meilleurs athlètes, certains venus de l’extérieur pour affiner leur préparation à ces échéances », a précisé Missiri Sawadogo. Le Burkina Faso qui a toujours dominé la compétition depuis son avènement en 2023, surtout dans les épreuves de haies et les épreuves techniques (sauts), sera représenté par 34 athlètes et cinq encadreurs. Ils seront menés par trois champions ou vice-champions d’Afrique de leurs catégories. Ce sont Zalissa Zongo, championne d’Afrique sortie de la catégorie U18 et championne de la région II récemment à Dakar avec des médailles dans les épreuves de longueur, triple saut et 100 m haies. Alan Sawadogo le meilleur junior au triple saut (plus de 16 m) et en longueur où il a obtenu la médaille d’or aux championnats juniors à Dakar en début juillet. Le 3e est Abdoul Rachid Traoré dont le record national au triple est de 15,33 m et de 14’’58 pour les 110m haies. Voro KORAHIRE

items.title: Prominent Indian Activist Breaks Hunger Strike After 26 Days
CHU de Tengandogo: un triple pontage coronarien réalisé avec succès
items.description: Le service de chirurgie thoracique et cardiovasculaire du CHU de Tengandogo a opéré avec succès un patient venu spécifiquement de la Côte d’Ivoire, son pays de résidence, pour subir un triple pontage coronarien. Réalisée, le 10 juillet 2026, cette intervention s’est faite en étroite collaboration avec l’équipe humanitaire italienne Safe Heart. L’opération a consisté en une revascularisation complète du coronaire gauche du patient. Pour y parvenir, les chirur-giens ont procédé à une double greffe délicate. Ils ont d’abord implanté une artère mammaire entre l’aorte et l’artère Inter-ventriculaire antérieure (IVA). Dans un second temps, l’équipe a prélevé la veine saphène droite du patient pour la brancher sur l’aorte, avant de la connecter aux deux artères marginales. Selon le premier bilan communiqué par les équipes médicales, l’intervention est une réussite totale et le patient se porte bien. Ce succès chirurgical n’est pas le fruit du hasard. Il découle d’un engagement constant des plus hautes autorités du pays, qui se sont investies à doter l’établissement d’un plateau technique moderne et adéquat, répondant aux stan-dards internationaux. Ce savoir- faire avait déjà été mis en lumière en décembre 2024 lors d’une première opération similaire. Au-delà de la performance tech- nique, cet événement confirme la transformation du Burkina Faso en une véritable destination sanitaire sur le continent. Le CHU de Tengandogo attire de plus en plus de patients extérieurs en quête de soins spécialisés. Ce flux de patients transfrontaliers est désormais régulier. On se rappelle qu’en 2022, onze enfants du Centre d’accueil Padre Pio de Bonoua (Côte d’Ivoire) avaient déjà été transférés et opérés à cœur ouvert au Burkina Faso. Aujourd’hui, l’expertise du CHU de Tengandogo séduit bien au-delà des résidents de Côte d’Ivoire et attire des malades venus du Mali, du Togo, du Nigeria et même du Burundi. Une montée en puissance qui redessine la carte de la prise en charge médicale en Afrique de l’Ouest. CHU de Tengandogo/Service communication
